output:
# Compute-Utility Routing for Sample-Adaptive Inference
Reframing the SHARCS adaptive-inference router (source paper) to close the Hardness-Confounded Compute Allocation gap.

## Research Analysis

### 1. Background and current state of the art

Adaptive and elastic inference has become the default lever for trading compute against accuracy in large pretrained models. Early-exit networks (BranchyNet, Teerapittayanon et al., 2016; Shallow-Deep, Kaya et al., 2019) and their transformer descendants (DeeBERT, Xin et al., 2020; FastBERT, Liu et al., 2020; PABEE, Zhou et al., 2020; BERxiT, Xin et al., 2021; CALM, Schuster et al., 2022) terminate computation per sample once an internal signal crosses a threshold. Elastic-width and nested architectures (DynaBERT, Hou et al., 2020; PowerBERT, Goyal et al., 2020; TranSkimmer, Guan et al., 2022; MatFormer, Devvrit et al., 2023) extend this to width and token budgets. SHARCS (source paper) is the most recent of this family: a lightweight router, trained on the base network's prediction confidence, assigns each sample a width/depth reduction factor in {0.25, 1.0} (we extend this to a finer grid {0.25, 0.5, 1.0}) and is scored by how well it orders samples by hardness (router-AUC 0.78 vs BERxiT 0.73 on RoBERTa-base, MNLI-m).

Across this entire literature, the routing decision reads off a single HARDNESS proxy -- confidence, entropy, or patience. Separately and without contact, the uncertainty-quantification literature has long DECOMPOSED predictive uncertainty into aleatoric (irreducible) and epistemic (model/compute-reducible) parts (deep ensembles, Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017; aleatoric/epistemic decomposition, Kendall & Gal, 2017; Depeweg et al., 2018; evidential deep learning, Sensoy et al., 2018; MC-dropout, Gal & Ghahramani, 2016; survey, Hullermeier & Waegeman, 2021). That decomposition, however, has only ever been pointed at active learning and OOD detection -- never at per-sample inference-compute allocation.

### 2. Key findings and analysis

The core finding is a confound we name Hardness-Confounded Compute Allocation (HCCA). A single hardness proxy conflates three distinct things: (i) EPISTEMIC difficulty, where more compute actually flips a prediction wrong->right and spending is justified; (ii) ALEATORIC difficulty, irreducible ambiguity or label noise where the full model is ALSO wrong, so any extra compute is burned; and (iii) the base model's MISCALIBRATION (Guo et al., 2017), which is worst precisely on hard and OOD samples -- exactly where the routing label is most wrong. A confidently-wrong sample exits early when more compute would have fixed it (accuracy lost), while an aleatorically-hard sample receives full compute that cannot help (compute wasted). The dataset-difficulty literature (dataset cartography, Swayamdipta et al., 2020; V-usable information, Ethayarajh et al., 2021, 2022; prediction depth, Baldock et al., 2021) characterizes WHICH samples are hard but never allocates inference compute by marginal utility.

This is not a synthetic edge case: it is real on SHARCS's own benchmark. MNLI carries documented human annotation disagreement (Pavlick & Kwiatkowski, 2019; ChaosNLI, Nie et al., 2020), a concrete aleatoric tail where the gold label itself is contested. Confidence routing cannot tell these irreducibly ambiguous items apart from compute-fixable ones, so it pays full price for both. Finally, the headline speedup is itself composition-confounded: a single accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto or router-AUC number silently bakes in one test-set easy/hard mixture, so part of any reported gain is the eval set's composition rather than the router's quality -- and it will not transfer under shift.

### 3. Novel insights or recommendations

The recommendation is to change the routing TARGET, not the architecture. Instead of routing on a hardness proxy, route on predicted MARGINAL COMPUTE-UTILITY, U(x) = P(correct | full compute) - P(correct | reduced compute): the per-sample correctness gain a marginal unit of compute actually buys. U(x) is supervised by a training-time multi-budget ORACLE SWEEP -- run each training example at every reduction factor, record the correctness vector, derive U directly -- forward passes that elastic/multi-exit training already executes, so the label is nearly free (modulo a small seed/mask-averaging factor used for noise control). Allocation becomes a per-batch KNAPSACK over predicted utility-density rather than a confidence threshold.

In parallel we recommend a measurement reform: report a composition-marginalized, utility-resolved efficiency profile that splits realized FLOPs into helped-compute, wasted-compute, and a named router-x-composition INTERACTION term. This profile collapses EXACTLY to SHARCS's published router-AUC/Pareto under boundary reduction B1 (confidence signal, fixed composition, decomposition off), so SHARCS is recovered as a strict special case while every new claim lives outside that boundary. We further recommend adopting per-subgroup FLOPs-allocation reporting (mean FLOPs per data slice at each budget) as a standing fairness standard, converting a worst-subgroup trip-wire into a transparent allocation report.

### 4. References and evidence

1. Devlin et al., 2018/2019 -- BERT.
2. Liu et al., 2019 -- RoBERTa.
3. Brown et al., 2020 -- GPT-3 / large LM compute cost.
4. Teerapittayanon et al., 2016 -- BranchyNet.
5. Kaya et al., 2019 -- Shallow-Deep Networks.
6. Xin et al., 2020 -- DeeBERT.
7. Liu et al., 2020 -- FastBERT.
8. Zhou et al., 2020 -- PABEE.
9. Xin et al., 2021 -- BERxiT.
10. Schuster et al., 2022 -- CALM.
11. Hou et al., 2020 -- DynaBERT.
12. Goyal et al., 2020 -- PowerBERT.
13. Guan et al., 2022 -- TranSkimmer.
14. Devvrit et al., 2023 -- MatFormer.
15. SHARCS (source paper) -- sample-adaptive inference via confidence-trained router.
16. Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017 -- deep ensembles.
17. Kendall & Gal, 2017 -- aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty in deep learning.
18. Depeweg et al., 2018 -- decomposition of uncertainty.
19. Hullermeier & Waegeman, 2021 -- aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty survey.
20. Sensoy et al., 2018 -- evidential deep learning.
21. Gal & Ghahramani, 2016 -- MC-dropout.
22. Guo et al., 2017 -- calibration of modern neural networks.
23. Swayamdipta et al., 2020 -- dataset cartography.
24. Ethayarajh et al., 2021/2022 -- V-usable information.
25. Baldock et al., 2021 -- prediction depth.
26. Pavlick & Kwiatkowski, 2019 -- inherent disagreement in NLI annotation.
27. Nie et al., 2020 -- ChaosNLI.
28. Chen et al., 2023 -- FrugalGPT.
29. Wang et al., 2019 -- GLUE benchmark.

## Proposed Research Idea (5q)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can sample-adaptive inference be made provably more efficient by routing compute on a router-predicted MARGINAL COMPUTE-UTILITY signal U(x)=P(correct|full)-P(correct|reduced) supervised by a training-time multi-budget oracle sweep and allocated via per-batch knapsack, instead of on confidence/entropy hardness proxies that conflate epistemic (compute-reducible) difficulty, aleatoric (irreducible) difficulty, and base-model miscalibration, and can the resulting speedup be honestly reported by a composition-marginalized, utility-resolved profile that splits realized FLOPs into helped-compute, wasted-compute, and a named router-x-composition interaction term, while collapsing exactly to SHARCS's published router-AUC/Pareto curve under boundary reduction B1?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The importance of this work is that it targets a PRIMITIVE, not a model. Hardness -- estimated as confidence, entropy, or patience -- is the default decision variable of an entire subfield: every early-exit and elastic-width router (BranchyNet (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016); Shallow-Deep (Kaya et al., 2019); DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2020); FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020); PABEE (Zhou et al., 2020); BERxiT (Xin et al., 2021); CALM (Schuster et al., 2022); DynaBERT (Hou et al., 2020); SHARCS) decides how much compute a sample receives by reading off a single hardness proxy. If that proxy is the wrong decision variable, then correcting it does not improve one architecture -- it re-bases the routing rule the whole subfield inherits. That is an unusually high-leverage target: a single change to the routing signal propagates to every method built on "hard == worth more compute."

Second, the work delivers a budget-neutral FREE LUNCH measured in the field's own currency. SHARCS's stated objective is to trade compute for efficiency in resource-limited settings. We show how to honor that objective correctly: at FIXED FLOPs and fixed parameters, compute spent on aleatorically-hard samples -- where the FULL model is also wrong -- is burned, because no budget flips the prediction. Reallocating that wasted-compute to epistemic-fixable samples (where more compute does flip wrong->right) raises accuracy at ZERO additional serving cost. This is not a new hardware budget or a bigger model; it is the same budget spent where the marginal return is positive. For deployers operating under hard latency and FLOPs ceilings, a method that lifts accuracy without raising the bill is directly actionable.

Third, the work produces a REUSABLE OBJECT, not a one-off score. The per-sample compute-utility curve U(x) = P(correct | full) - P(correct | reduced) is a first-class artifact: it states, per example, how much a marginal unit of compute buys. Once estimated, U(x) is reusable far beyond this paper -- for FLOPs budgeting, for deferral and cascade routing in the FrugalGPT (Chen et al., 2023) sense, for serving-cost accounting, and for model-cascade design. Where prior routers emit a transient threshold tuned to one operating point, U(x) is a portable quantity other systems can consume.

Fourth, the work is a durable BENCHMARKING reform. We argue that a single scalar Pareto/AUC is composition-dependent: re-weighting the test set's easy/hard mixture moves the number with the router held fixed, so part of every published adaptive-inference speedup is test-set composition, not method quality. The composition-marginalized, utility-resolved profile fixes this by splitting realized FLOPs into helped-compute, wasted-compute, and a named router-x-composition INTERACTION term. The interaction term is the contribution: it isolates exactly the portion of an apparent speedup that is an artifact of who was in the test set. Just as introducing an explicit distortion axis reframed "we compressed it" into an incomplete claim, the helped/wasted decomposition turns "we got a speedup" into a claim that is incomplete until it states FOR WHICH composition. This is a measurement standard the subfield can adopt independently of whether they adopt our router.

Finally, the work is built for the deployment regime SHARCS itself motivates. Resource-limited serving is precisely where distribution shift bites, and confidence routing degrades SILENTLY under shift: when composition changes, a confidence threshold calibrated on the source mix mis-allocates on the target mix without any visible signal. Utility routing is designed to survive composition change because U(x) grades "would compute help this sample," a quantity that is more stable under re-weighting than the easy/hard mixture itself. The same property that makes the efficiency report honest (composition-marginalization) makes the deployed router robust -- so the contribution is simultaneously a scientific correction and a practical hardening of adaptive inference for the OOD, resource-constrained setting that is the original motivation.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The central obstacle is that compute-utility is a COUNTERFACTUAL quantity that is unobservable at inference time. The signal we want to route on, U(x) = P(correct | full compute) - P(correct | reduced compute), requires knowing what the SAME sample x would do under two different compute budgets, but at test time each sample is processed under exactly one allocation. You never get to see both branches of the counterfactual for a single test point, so U(x) cannot be measured online and must be PREDICTED. This is precisely why the SHARCS family (source paper) and its predecessors (DeeBERT, Xin et al. 2020; BERxiT, Xin et al. 2021; PABEE, Zhou et al. 2020; FastBERT, Liu et al. 2020) fall back on confidence/entropy: confidence IS observable at inference from a single forward pass, so it is a convenient proxy for hardness. The instrument that makes the counterfactual tractable is a TRAINING-TIME multi-budget oracle sweep: at training time we have labels, so we can run each training example at every reduction factor in {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} (and at each early-exit depth), record a per-budget correctness vector, and derive U(x) directly as a realized correctness-flip. Crucially this is cheap and largely already paid for: elastic/multi-exit training (DynaBERT, Hou et al. 2020; MatFormer, Devvrit et al. 2023; CALM, Schuster et al. 2022) ALREADY executes the sub-network forward passes during training, so harvesting correctness at each budget is a one-time training-data bookkeeping cost, not a new inference cost.

Why can a naive "route on confidence then calibrate" NOT recover utility? Because calibration only fixes the MAP from a score to a probability of correctness AT A FIXED BUDGET; it makes P(correct | full, conf) well-estimated, but it says nothing about the DIFFERENCE across budgets. Two samples can have identical low confidence yet opposite utility: an epistemically-hard sample (the reduced model is confused but the full model gets it right -> U high, spend justified) and an aleatorically-hard sample (irreducibly ambiguous; the full model is ALSO wrong -> U ~ 0, spend wasted). Confidence, even perfectly calibrated, is a monotone function of a single-budget hardness proxy and is blind to the cross-budget flip. Post-hoc temperature scaling (Guo et al., 2017) cannot manufacture the second budget's information that was never in the score. Routing more compute to all low-confidence samples therefore systematically wastes compute on the aleatoric tail. This is the operational content of the Hardness-Confounded Compute Allocation gap: hardness conflates epistemic difficulty (compute-reducible), aleatoric difficulty (irreducible), and base-model miscalibration, and the reported speedup is further confounded by TEST-SET COMPOSITION (how many easy vs hard samples the benchmark happens to contain).

A second difficulty is conceptual: the aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty split is theoretically CONTESTED. Hullermeier and Waegeman (2021) show the decomposition is model- and representation-dependent and lacks a unique ground truth; UQ methods that target it (Kendall and Gal, 2017; Depeweg et al., 2018; Sensoy et al., 2018; Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) disagree on definitions. We SIDESTEP the philosophy entirely. We never claim to measure metaphysical aleatoric uncertainty. We define utility OPERATIONALLY as the realized correctness-flip across compute budgets -- a directly observable, label-grounded quantity at training time. "Aleatoric" in our usage is just shorthand for "U(x) ~ 0 because the full model is also wrong"; "epistemic" is shorthand for "U(x) > 0 because more compute flips wrong->right." This reframing is the key move: it converts an unfalsifiable decomposition debate into a measurable regression target, and it is what lets B4 inject KNOWN-aleatoric (label-flipped, irreducible) and KNOWN-epistemic (correct only at full compute) samples and CHECK that the decomposition recovers the planted split.

A third difficulty is LABEL NOISE in the oracle itself. A sample may flip to "correct" by luck at a given budget (e.g., a stochastic dropout mask or a borderline logit lands favorably), so a single sweep produces a noisy correctness vector and an inflated or deflated U(x). The instrument is to average over stochasticity -- multiple seeds and/or dropout masks per budget, or, better, use EXPECTED correctness via the predicted probability of the gold label rather than a hard 0/1 argmax -- and then threshold U by significance so we only act on flips that exceed the estimated noise floor. This prevents the router from being trained to chase phantom utility.

A fourth difficulty is that the router can GAME any aggregate utility metric. If we simply maximize summed predicted utility under a budget, the router learns to dump compute on cheap-to-flip samples to inflate the average while starving (and tanking) a worst-case subgroup. The instrument is a per-batch KNAPSACK over predicted utility PLUS an explicit worst-subgroup accuracy guardrail at fixed budget: the allocation must not let any reported subgroup fall below the confidence-routing baseline at equal FLOPs. This makes "do no harm" a constraint, not a hope.

A fifth difficulty is that FLOPs are NOT wall-clock latency. Width reduction to 0.25 does not give a 4x speedup on real hardware (memory bandwidth, kernel launch overhead, poor utilization of narrow GEMMs, and per-sample heterogeneous batching all interfere), and the router and the knapsack add their own overhead. The instrument is to REPORT realized wall-clock latency alongside theoretical FLOPs on fixed hardware, and to explicitly charge the router's forward cost and the allocation cost to the budget, so the efficiency claim is not a paper-FLOPs artifact.

A sixth difficulty is DISTRIBUTION SHIFT, which moves BOTH the test-set composition AND the utility function itself (the same surface form can be epistemically hard in-domain and aleatorically hard out-of-domain). The instrument is twofold: evaluate under controlled composition re-weighting (B2) to isolate composition effects, and under OOD/cross-dataset splits (e.g., MNLI -> SNLI, or matched -> mismatched) to test U-predictor transfer; where transfer degrades, train the utility predictor with shift-robust objectives. This is also why the evaluation must MARGINALIZE over composition: a scalar Pareto/AUC number silently bakes in one test mix.

A seventh difficulty is that the per-batch knapsack is formally NP-hard and must nonetheless run online and cheaply. The instrument is a greedy approximation that sorts samples by utility DENSITY (predicted U per incremental FLOP) and fills the budget top-down; for the 0/1-knapsack with a single FLOPs constraint this greedy rule -- in its modified form (take the better of density-ordered fill and the single most valuable feasible item) -- has the classic 1/2-approximation guarantee (and matches the LP relaxation up to one fractional item), runs in O(n log n) per batch, and adds negligible overhead. We state this bound explicitly and verify empirically that greedy tracks the exact-DP allocation within tolerance on held-out batches.

Taken together: the hardness is that the routing signal is counterfactual (-> training-time oracle sweep, cheap because elastic passes already exist), the decomposition it relies on is contested (-> operational correctness-flip definition), the oracle is noisy (-> average + significance threshold), the metric is gameable (-> knapsack + worst-subgroup guardrail), FLOPs lie about latency (-> measure wall-clock and charge router cost), shift moves the target (-> composition re-weighting + OOD splits + robust training), and exact allocation is intractable (-> greedy utility-density with a provable bound). Each obstacle is paired with a concrete, runnable instrument, and none is swept under the rug.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

It has not been solved because it falls in the SEAM between two literatures that grade different objects and never crossed this axis. On one side, the adaptive-inference and early-exit literature -- BranchyNet (Teerapittayanon et al., 2016); Shallow-Deep (Kaya et al., 2019); DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2020); FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020); PABEE (Zhou et al., 2020); BERxiT (Xin et al., 2021); CALM (Schuster et al., 2022); DynaBERT (Hou et al., 2020); PowerBERT (Goyal et al., 2020); MatFormer (Devvrit et al., 2023); SHARCS -- decides COMPUTE allocation, but reads it off a confidence/entropy/patience HARDNESS proxy. On the other side, the uncertainty-quantification literature DECOMPOSES uncertainty into aleatoric and epistemic parts (deep ensembles -- Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017; aleatoric/epistemic decomposition -- Kendall & Gal, 2017; Depeweg et al., 2018; Hullermeier & Waegeman, 2021; evidential deep learning -- Sensoy et al., 2018; MC-dropout -- Gal & Ghahramani, 2016), but it applied that decomposition to active learning and OOD detection -- never to per-sample inference-COMPUTE allocation. One field knew how to split uncertainty but pointed it at data selection; the other field allocated compute but used an undecomposed hardness scalar. The cross-product -- decomposed uncertainty driving compute allocation -- was simply never instantiated.

Why is each prior adaptive-inference method confidence-bound by construction? Because all of them DERIVE the routing label from a hardness proxy. SHARCS trains its router on the base network's confidence; BERxiT (Xin et al., 2021) and DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2020) exit on entropy/confidence; PABEE (Zhou et al., 2020) exits on cross-layer patience; CALM (Schuster et al., 2022) thresholds a confidence measure. Whatever their architectural differences, they share one supervised target -- "is this sample hard?" -- and therefore ALL inherit the same defect: hardness conflates (i) epistemic difficulty (compute flips wrong->right; spending justified), (ii) aleatoric difficulty (irreducible ambiguity/label noise; the full model is also wrong; compute wasted), and (iii) base-model MISCALIBRATION, which is worst exactly where it hurts most -- over-confidence concentrated on hard/OOD samples, where the routing label itself is most wrong. The dataset-difficulty literature (dataset cartography -- Swayamdipta et al., 2020; V-usable information -- Ethayarajh et al., 2021, 2022; prediction depth -- Baldock et al., 2021) CHARACTERIZES which samples are hard, but it too stops at difficulty and never allocates inference compute by marginal utility. So the UNCLAIMED QUADRANT is specific: epistemic-utility-driven adaptive computation -- routing on "would more compute HELP this sample?" rather than "is this sample HARD?". No prior method occupies it.

We pre-empt the three objections that make this look less novel than it is. (a) "Isn't this just early-exit / SHARCS with a better threshold?" No. Early-exit thresholds a hardness PROXY; we supervise a COUNTERFACTUAL marginal-utility target, U(x) = P(correct | full) - P(correct | reduced), measured by a training-time multi-budget oracle sweep, and we allocate by per-batch KNAPSACK rather than a confidence cutoff. Different signal (utility, not hardness), different supervision (counterfactual oracle sweep, not the base model's own confidence), different allocation (knapsack, not threshold), and a different, falsifiable claim. (b) "Isn't this just calibration (Guo et al., 2017)?" No. Calibration corrects confidence MAGNITUDES, but a perfectly calibrated confidence STILL routes on hardness -- and still spends full compute on an aleatorically-hard sample where the full model is also wrong. Calibration cannot SEPARATE epistemic from aleatoric difficulty; utility can, because U(x) is near zero precisely when compute does not change the outcome, regardless of how well-calibrated the confidence is. (c) "Isn't aleatoric/epistemic decomposition already known?" Yes, in UQ (Kendall & Gal, 2017; Depeweg et al., 2018; Hullermeier & Waegeman, 2021) -- but it was never operationalized as REALIZED correctness-flip across compute budgets for inference allocation. We deliberately sidestep the contested metaphysics of the split (Hullermeier & Waegeman, 2021) with an operational, measurable definition: utility is the empirical change in correctness when budget changes, observable from the oracle sweep, not a philosophical quantity.

Crucially, we GENERALIZE rather than contradict prior work. Under boundary reduction B1 -- routing signal set to confidence, test composition held fixed, decomposition disabled -- the framework collapses by construction to the confidence-routing special case (a unit-testable equality in our own codebase), reproducing SHARCS's published router-AUC (about 0.78) and accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto curve within reproduction tolerance. SHARCS is recovered as the confidence-signal, fixed-composition, no-decomposition special case of compute-utility routing; everything new lives strictly outside that boundary. This is what makes the novelty positioned rather than asserted: we name the quadrant we occupy, show the prior method as our degenerate case, and place our claim where no nesting can absorb it.

Finally, the gap is real on SHARCS's OWN benchmark, not a synthetic edge case. MNLI carries documented annotation disagreement -- human labelers genuinely diverge on a non-trivial fraction of pairs (Pavlick & Kwiatkowski, 2019; Nie et al., 2020, ChaosNLI). Those samples are a concrete source of ALEATORIC difficulty: they read as "hard" to a confidence proxy, so confidence routing spends full compute on them, yet the full model cannot resolve an item whose gold label is itself contested -- the compute is burned. Utility routing detects U(x) ~ 0 on exactly these items and stops paying for them. That confidence routing cannot tell this irreducible difficulty apart from compute-fixable difficulty -- on the very dataset SHARCS reports -- is the clearest evidence that the Hardness-Confounded Compute Allocation gap is genuine and unaddressed.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

Models and data (matched to SHARCS for direct comparability). We use RoBERTa-base (Liu et al., 2019) and BERT-base (Devlin et al., 2019) as the base networks, exactly as in SHARCS and BERxiT, so our B1 numbers are directly comparable to SHARCS's published router-AUC of 0.78 (RoBERTa-base, MNLI-m) versus BERxiT's 0.73. We evaluate on GLUE (Wang et al., 2019): MNLI-m and MNLI-mm, QQP, SST-2, QNLI, RTE, MRPC, and CoLA. Adaptive compute uses WIDTH reduction factors {0.25, 0.5, 1.0} and adaptive DEPTH via early-exit layers, mirroring SHARCS's adaptive width/depth. We deliberately foreground MNLI's documented human-disagreement as a real, named aleatoric source: ChaosNLI (Nie et al., 2020) provides 100-annotator label distributions on a subset of MNLI/SNLI, giving us samples with genuinely irreducible ambiguity. These are gold for B4: items where human label entropy is high are expected to have U(x) ~ 0 (full compute cannot fix what humans themselves disagree on), so they are natural KNOWN-aleatoric injections.

Utility labels. At training time we run the multi-budget oracle sweep: each training example is evaluated at every reduction factor and early-exit depth, producing a correctness vector; we aggregate over seeds/dropout masks (or use expected gold-label probability) and derive U(x) = P(correct | full) - P(correct | reduced), thresholded by significance. We then train a LIGHTWEIGHT router of the SAME capacity as SHARCS's router (a small MLP head on a base-network hidden state) for fairness -- the comparison must not be confounded by router size. We contrast two router objectives: a REGRESSION head (predict U directly, MSE/Huber) versus a RANKING objective (pairwise/listwise, predict the ORDER of samples by utility), since allocation only needs the ordering of utility-density and ranking losses are often more robust to U's noisy scale.

Allocation. At inference we do per-batch knapsack via utility density: sort by predicted U per incremental FLOP and greedily promote samples to higher budgets until the global FLOPs budget for the batch is spent (greedy bound discussed in Q3). Sweeping the global budget from aggressive (favoring 0.25) to conservative (favoring 1.0) traces the full accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto. This replaces SHARCS's per-sample confidence thresholding with a budget-coupled, composition-aware allocation.

Baselines. (1) SHARCS (source paper) -- the primary comparison. (2) Confidence/entropy-threshold routing -- the mechanism SHARCS and the field use. (3) BERxiT (Xin et al., 2021), DeeBERT (Xin et al., 2020), PABEE (Zhou et al., 2020), FastBERT (Liu et al., 2020) -- adaptive-inference field baselines. (4) RANDOM allocation -- a mechanism null: a non-informative router spending the same FLOPs must show NO gain; if "Compute-Utility Routing" beats random only as much as random beats nothing, the mechanism is empty. (5) HOMOGENEOUS-utility regime -- a constructed/filtered split where all samples are equally fixable (U roughly constant); here utility routing must show NO gain over uniform allocation, proving gains come from utility HETEROGENEITY, not from the apparatus. (6) ORACLE-utility upper bound -- route with ground-truth U from the sweep (cheating ceiling); this caps achievable performance and quantifies the predictor's headroom.

Metrics. (1) Standard accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto and router-AUC (samples ordered by hardness), for B1 comparability to SHARCS's 0.78. (2) NEW wasted-compute fraction (FLOPs spent on samples with U ~ 0) and helped-compute fraction (FLOPs on U > 0 samples that flipped wrong->right). (3) Compute-utility ALIGNMENT = Kendall-tau between predicted utility and oracle utility (and, as a contrast, between CONFIDENCE and oracle utility -- we expect the latter to be low). (4) Worst-subgroup accuracy at fixed budget (the gaming guardrail). (5) Realized wall-clock latency on fixed hardware alongside theoretical FLOPs, charging router cost. (6) The composition-marginalized, utility-resolved profile across re-weighted test compositions (B2), splitting realized FLOPs into helped-compute / wasted-compute / router-x-composition INTERACTION. (7) Per-subgroup FLOPs allocation (mean FLOPs per MNLI genre slice: telephone, fiction, government, travel, slate) at each budget point, converting the worst-subgroup guardrail from a trip-wire into an allocation-fairness report.

Falsifier ladder (stated explicitly, rungs in order). B1 ORACLE/EXACT COLLAPSE: with confidence signal + fixed composition + decomposition off, our system must collapse exactly by construction to the confidence-routing special case in our own codebase (a unit-testable equality) and reproduce SHARCS's published router-AUC (about 0.78) and accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto within reproduction tolerance; if it does not, our reframing is wrong before we start. B2 PLACEBO / COMPOSITION SWAP: freeze the router and re-weight the test easy/hard mix; the scalar Pareto/AUC MUST move (demonstrating those metrics are composition-confounded) while the utility-resolved profile MUST stay stable. FAILURE CONDITION (falsifier): if the resolved profile moves beyond the synthetic-injection tolerance under PURE composition re-weighting, the central claim that the profile is composition-marginalized is FALSIFIED. B3 UTILITY-RESOLVED DIVERSE PROFILE: with predicted U + knapsack, emit helped/wasted/interaction across all datasets and budgets. B4 ORACLE CEILING + INJECTION RECOVERY: route with ground-truth oracle U (ceiling) and inject KNOWN-aleatoric (ChaosNLI-high-entropy / label-flipped, irreducible) and KNOWN-epistemic (correct only at full compute) samples; the decomposition MUST recover the planted wasted/helped split within tolerance, else the localization is unreliable. MECHANISM NULLS: RANDOM and HOMOGENEOUS-utility must predict NO gain. DO-NO-HARM: utility routing must never fall below confidence routing at equal budget, and on aleatoric-free data it must TIE confidence routing (when there is no irreducible tail, there is nothing for utility to save).

Expected outcomes (made clear, with defensible magnitudes). We expect utility routing to DOMINATE confidence routing on the accuracy-vs-FLOPs Pareto frontier, with the LARGEST gains in the AGGRESSIVE-efficiency regime (the 0.25 reduction-factor / low-budget end, where every misallocated FLOP hurts) and on NOISY/AMBIGUOUS datasets (MNLI with its ChaosNLI tail, CoLA, RTE) where the aleatoric fraction is non-trivial; on clean, easy datasets (SST-2) we expect a TIE, consistent with do-no-harm. Concretely we expect order-1-to-3 accuracy points improvement at matched FLOPs in the aggressive regime (a defensible, not heroic, magnitude given SHARCS's own 0.78 vs 0.73 AUC margins), shrinking toward zero as the budget approaches full compute. We expect a measurable REDUCTION in wasted-compute fraction (compute redirected away from the U ~ 0 aleatoric tail toward U > 0 flippable samples), Kendall-tau(predicted, oracle utility) HIGH (e.g., >= 0.5-0.7) while Kendall-tau(confidence, oracle utility) is markedly LOWER -- the quantitative heart of the HCCA argument. We expect the B2 composition-swap placebo to show that a QUANTIFIABLE fraction of SHARCS's apparent gain is attributable to favorable test-set composition rather than routing quality (the scalar AUC moves under re-weighting while our resolved profile holds). FALSIFIERS of the central claim, stated plainly: (a) if Kendall-tau(predicted utility, oracle) is NOT meaningfully higher than Kendall-tau(confidence, oracle), then utility carries no information beyond confidence and the thesis fails; (b) if wasted-compute fraction does NOT drop relative to confidence routing at matched FLOPs, the mechanism does nothing; (c) if the utility-resolved profile MOVES under pure B2 composition re-weighting beyond injection tolerance, the composition-marginalization claim is false; (d) if utility routing ever falls BELOW confidence routing at equal budget, do-no-harm is violated. Any one of these refutes the proposal, which is exactly the property a real methodology should have.

## Limitations and Responsible-AI Notes

This proposal is a measurement and routing-signal reform, not a universal accuracy cure, and several limitations bound its claims. The oracle sweep grounds utility only as well as the training labels and the chosen reduction-factor grid; coarse budgets, noisy labels, or a base model that is wrong at ALL budgets leave U(x) ~ 0 and offer the router nothing to exploit, so gains are expected only where utility is heterogeneous and the aleatoric tail is non-trivial. The aleatoric/epistemic framing is operational (realized correctness-flip across budgets), not a metaphysical decomposition, and should not be read as a claim about true data uncertainty. FLOPs are reported alongside realized wall-clock latency precisely because the two diverge on real hardware, and all efficiency claims charge the router and allocation overhead to the budget.

Two responsible-AI folds sharpen the scope. First, utility U(x) is MODEL-RELATIVE: it is defined against the base model's own correctness, so it inherits the base model's distributional biases -- a sample the base model is systematically wrong about (for a minority dialect, register, or topic) can register low utility simply because no available budget fixes the base model's blind spot. We therefore inspect ABSOLUTE per-slice accuracy for minority inputs separately, not only worst-case subgroup accuracy at fixed budget; a budget-neutral average gain must never be allowed to mask an absolute regression on an under-served slice, and the per-subgroup FLOPs-allocation report (Metric 7) is the standing instrument for surfacing such allocation skew. Second, all aggregate claims are SCOPED to English natural-language-understanding benchmarks (GLUE, MNLI/ChaosNLI); we make no claim about multilingual, code, generative, or long-context settings, where both the composition structure and the shape of the utility function may differ -- those are explicitly future work, not implied by the present results.
